children.children: What if a language model's 7 consensus neurons aren't just a binary 'normal vs. abnormal' switch — but a 7-bit fingerprint that tells you WHAT KIND of expectation failure just happened? This tutorial connects GPT-2's routing architecture to Schank's failure-driven memory and modern event segmentation research.

Roger Schank argued in Dynamic Memory (1982) that human memory is failure-driven: you don't remember the 100 routine restaurant visits, you remember the one where the waiter dropped your soup. Memory formation is triggered by expectation failures — moments when your running script is violated.

In GPT-2 Small's final MLP, we found 7 consensus neurons that each monitor a different dimension of linguistic 'normality.' When all 7 agree (high consensus), the model is in familiar territory. When consensus breaks down, the exception handler fires. But here's the key insight: because we have 7 independent monitors, we don't just know THAT an expectation failed — we know WHICH KIND of expectation failed.

Imagine a building's fire alarm panel with 7 zone indicators: Lobby, Kitchen, Office, Server Room, Garage, Rooftop, Basement. When all 7 are green, everything's fine. A single scalar 'fire alarm' just tells you 'there's a problem somewhere.' But 7 independent zone monitors tell you WHERE the problem is. A Kitchen alarm triggers the sprinkler system. A Server Room alarm triggers halon gas. Different failure types demand different responses. The consensus neurons work the same way: each monitors a different linguistic zone, and the dropout pattern tells you what kind of failure occurred.
Each consensus neuron monitors a specific linguistic dimension. They fire on 79-91% of tokens (they're 'on' by default — monitoring normality), and each has a distinct specialization:

children.definition: Fires mid-clause on connectives like 'and', 'but', 'also'. Silent at clause boundaries. Monitors: Is the sentence continuing as expected?
Fires on subordinators like 'that', 'while', 'neither'. Depleted on sentence-initial positions. Monitors: Is the grammar getting more complex in a predictable way?
Fires on prepositions and relational words: 'an', 'into', 'per'. Monitors: Are entities being related to each other normally?
Fires on ordinals, lists, parallel structure. Highest mean activation. Monitors: Is the discourse organized in a predictable sequence?
Fires on topic-continuing phrases. 77K 'disagreement' tokens. Monitors: Is the topic staying consistent?
Fires on words marking semantic roles: 'leadership', 'validity', 'one of the'. Monitors: Are the thematic roles (agent, patient, instrument) in order?
Fires on currencies, dates, proper names. Depleted on abstract adjectives like 'natural', 'social'. Monitors: Are we talking about something concrete and specific?

children.children: Six neurons (N2–N1245) form a 'linguistic structure axis' with cosine similarities 0.52–0.73. N2600 is nearly orthogonal (cos ≈ 0.0) — it forms a separate 'referential concreteness axis.' Full consensus requires BOTH structural predictability AND referential concreteness.

props.title: From Scalar Surprise to Typed Failure

children.children: Kumar et al. (2023) showed that GPT-2's surprisal (how 'surprised' the model is at each token) predicts where humans perceive event boundaries in stories. High surprisal = event boundary. But surprisal is a single number — it tells you HOW MUCH surprise, not WHAT KIND.
Our 7 consensus neurons decompose that scalar into 7 independent channels. When surprisal spikes, we can ask: which monitors dropped out?
Consider three different high-surprisal tokens and their hypothesized consensus patterns:

Text: 'The committee discussed the implications of the $47.3 million...'
Token: '$47.3' → Concrete, specific, but syntactically unusual mid-clause.

Expected pattern: N2600 (concrete reference) stays ON. N2 (clausal continuation) might DROP. Fingerprint: structural failure, not referential. The sentence structure is disrupted by an embedded quantity, but the referent is maximally concrete.

Text: 'She walked, and walked, and walked, and then —'
Token: '—' at a disruption of the parallel structure.

Expected pattern: N2928 (sequential structure) DROPS — the parallel pattern is broken. N1831 (discourse coherence) DROPS — topic is shifting. But N2600 (concrete reference) was already low (no specific referents). Fingerprint: structural + discourse failure.

Text: 'The meeting adjourned at 3pm. §§ CLASSIFIED §§'
Token: '§§' → All seven monitors drop simultaneously.

This is a complete script violation — nothing about the token is linguistically predictable. The exception handler fires at maximum intensity. In Schank's terms, this isn't a script modification; it's a script abandonment requiring a completely new frame.

props.title: Consensus Dropout Simulator
props.description: Toggle each consensus neuron on/off to see how the failure fingerprint changes. Watch how different dropout patterns map to different types of linguistic expectation failure.

children.children: Roger Schank's Dynamic Memory (1982, revised 1999) proposed three key ideas that connect directly to our consensus architecture:

children.definition: You remember events that violate expectations. The restaurant where everything went normally? Forgotten. The one where the waiter insulted you? Seared into memory. Memory formation is triggered by prediction error.

Expectations are organized hierarchically. A 'restaurant MOP' contains sub-scripts (enter, sit, order, eat, pay, leave). Failure at any level triggers different responses — a wrong dish triggers a sub-script fix, but a fire triggers MOP abandonment.

After a failure, you build an explanation and generalize it. 'This restaurant is bad' generalizes differently from 'all restaurants on this street are bad.' The TYPE of failure determines the scope of generalization.

children.children: The direct mapping: Consensus neurons = MOP monitors. Each tracks whether a sub-script is running normally. 7/7 consensus = all sub-scripts nominal. Dropout of N2928 (sequential structure) = the 'ordering' sub-script was violated. Dropout of N2600 (concrete reference) = we've shifted from concrete to abstract discourse, like moving from ordering food to discussing philosophy. Different failures, different repair primitives.

props.title: Event Segmentation: Where Psychology Meets Neurons

children.children: Zacks and Tversky's Event Segmentation Theory (2001) showed that humans automatically divide continuous experience into discrete events. Event boundaries occur at prediction error spikes — moments when what happens next is different from what you expected.

Kumar et al. (2023) demonstrated that GPT-2's token-level surprisal predicts where humans perceive event boundaries in stories. Bayesian surprise (KL divergence between the model's posterior and prior) was a better predictor than raw surprisal — it captures not just 'how surprising' but 'how much the model's beliefs changed.'

Here's the conceptual leap: Kumar et al. used a single scalar (surprisal or Bayesian surprise) to predict event boundaries. But our consensus decomposition turns that scalar into a 7-dimensional vector. Each dimension is an independent expectation channel.
This predicts that human event segmentation should be richer than 'boundary vs. non-boundary.' There should be TYPES of boundaries:

• Referential shift (N2600 drops): 'John left the room. Meanwhile, in Tokyo...' — the concrete referent changes
• Structural break (N2928 drops): 'First, second, third — wait, actually...' — the sequential pattern is disrupted
• Discourse pivot (N1831 drops): 'The economy grew. But what about the climate?' — topic coherence breaks
• Argument reframe (N1245 drops): 'The CEO praised the intern' vs 'The intern praised the CEO' — role assignment shifts

If this is right, different boundary types should correlate with different Schankian primitives at the boundary: PTRANS events lose spatial/sequential neurons, MTRANS events lose information-flow neurons, ATRANS events lose argument-structure neurons.

With 7 binary monitors, there are 2⁷ = 128 possible consensus patterns. In practice, most tokens cluster at high consensus (7/7 = 38% of tokens, 6/7 = 34%). But the low-consensus patterns — the failure fingerprints — are where the interesting structure lives.

props.title: Failure Fingerprint Distribution
props.description: Explore the distribution of consensus levels and what happens at each level. The MLP goes from helpful to harmful as consensus increases — the crossover is statistically sharp between 4/7 and 5/7.

props.title: Connecting the Dots: Typed Failure → Primitive Selection

children.children: The key prediction: different consensus dropout patterns should trigger different Schankian repair primitives. This gives us a testable framework:

FAILURE FINGERPRINT           → PREDICTED PRIMITIVE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────

N2600 drops, others hold       → MTRANS (information transfer)
  "The theory implies that..."    Lost concrete grounding,
                                   shifting to abstract info flow

N2928 drops, others hold       → PTRANS (physical transfer)
  "He walked into the room"      Sequential structure disrupted
                                   by physical movement event

N1245 drops, others hold       → ATRANS (possession transfer)
  "She gave him the book"         Argument structure shifts
                                   (new agent-patient relation)

N1831 + N2 drop, others hold   → Script boundary (MOP switch)
  "Anyway, let me tell you about" Discourse coherence breaks,
                                   clause continuation disrupted

All 7 drop simultaneously      → Script abandonment
  Rare tokens, subwords,          Complete expectation collapse;
  paragraph breaks                 requires full re-contextualization

These mappings are HYPOTHESIZED, not yet tested. The experimental design is straightforward: annotate WikiText-103 tokens with Schankian primitives (using the operator classifier from our wake-sleep work), record consensus patterns, and compute contingency tables. If the correlation is significant, the consensus architecture IS a typed primitive dispatcher.

The Hydra architecture has multiple heads, each monitoring a different linguistic dimension — phrase structure, thematic fit, information structure, plus a blank discovery head. This is the consensus decomposition made EXPLICIT and LEARNABLE.

In GPT-2, the 7 consensus neurons emerged implicitly from training. They weren't designed to monitor specific dimensions — they converged on those roles because monitoring multiple independent channels of 'normality' is the optimal strategy for the terminal MLP's routing problem.

Hydra takes this implicit emergence and makes it architectural: each head is an explicit expectation monitor with a known specialization. When the thematic head detects a failure, the system knows it needs a thematic repair (not a structural one). This should produce sharper, more efficient routing than GPT-2's implicit version.

Hydra's fourth head has no labels — it learns its own dimension during training. The consensus decomposition suggests what it might discover: the gap between our 7 known dimensions and the full space of expectation types.

N2600 (concrete reference) is already qualitatively different from the other 6 (orthogonal output direction). The blank head might discover another orthogonal dimension — perhaps temporal structure, emotional valence, or register/formality. Whatever it finds, it becomes a new channel in the typed failure fingerprint, increasing the resolution of the primitive dispatch table.

props.question: In Schank's Dynamic Memory, what triggers memory formation?
props.explanation: Schank's core claim is that memory is failure-driven: you remember events that violate your expectations (scripts), not routine ones. This maps directly to low-consensus tokens in our framework — moments when the model's expectation monitors disagree.

props.question: At what consensus level does the MLP crossover from helpful to harmful?
props.explanation: The crossover is statistically sharp between 4/7 (MLP helps, ΔP = +0.014) and 5/7 (MLP harms, ΔP = -0.004). All bootstrap 95% CIs exclude zero at both levels. Below the crossover, the exception handler is doing useful work; above it, the model would be better off skipping the MLP entirely.

props.question: What makes N2600 different from the other 6 consensus neurons?
options: It has a much lower fire rate
It monitors concrete reference (currencies, dates, names) and is orthogonal to the others
It is causally necessary for the routing program
It only fires during exception-path processing
props.explanation: N2600 has cosine similarity ≈ 0.0 with all other consensus neurons, forming a separate 'referential concreteness axis.' The other 6 have pairwise cosine similarities of 0.52–0.73 (aligned but not redundant). Full consensus requires BOTH structural predictability AND referential concreteness — two independent conditions.

props.question: Kumar et al. (2023) used GPT-2 surprisal to predict human event boundaries. How does the consensus decomposition extend their finding?
options: It replaces surprisal with a more accurate scalar measure
It decomposes scalar surprisal into 7 independent channels, enabling typed event boundaries
It shows that GPT-2 doesn't actually predict event boundaries
It proves that human event segmentation uses exactly 7 dimensions
props.explanation: Surprisal tells you HOW MUCH surprise; the 7-bit consensus fingerprint tells you WHAT KIND. Different dropout patterns correspond to different types of expectation failure (referential, structural, discourse, etc.), predicting that event boundaries should have types, not just magnitudes.

children.children: This idea is directly testable with existing infrastructure. Here's the experiment design:

# Pseudocode for the key experiment

# 1. Annotate WikiText-103 tokens with Schankian primitives
#    (use the operator classifier from wake-sleep work)
for sentence in wikitext_103:
    events = extract_events(sentence)  # SRL or event detector
    for event in events:
        primitive = classify_primitive(event)  # ATRANS/PTRANS/MTRANS/...
        for token in event.tokens:
            token.primitive = primitive

# 2. Record consensus patterns at each token
for token in all_tokens:
    consensus_pattern = [n.fires(token) for n in consensus_neurons]  # 7-bit vector
    token.consensus = sum(consensus_pattern)
    token.fingerprint = tuple(consensus_pattern)

# 3. Compute contingency tables
import scipy.stats as stats
contingency = build_contingency_table(
    rows=unique_fingerprints,     # 128 possible patterns
    cols=unique_primitives,        # ATRANS, PTRANS, MTRANS, MBUILD, ...
    values=token_counts
)
chi2, p, dof, expected = stats.chi2_contingency(contingency)
print(f'Chi-squared: {chi2:.1f}, p={p:.2e}, dof={dof}')

# 4. If significant: which neurons predict which primitives?
for neuron in consensus_neurons:
    for primitive in primitives:
        enrichment = compute_enrichment(neuron.dropout_tokens, primitive_tokens)
        print(f'{neuron.id} dropout -> {primitive}: {enrichment:.1f}x enrichment')

If the chi-squared test is significant and specific neurons predict specific primitives, we have strong evidence that the consensus architecture IS a typed primitive dispatcher — connecting mechanistic interpretability (Darkness Visible) to cognitive science (Schank's Dynamic Memory) to computational neuroscience (Event Segmentation Theory).

The 7 consensus neurons in GPT-2's final MLP don't just signal 'normal vs. abnormal' — they provide a typed expectation failure fingerprint that structurally implements Schank's failure-driven memory. Different dropout patterns identify different KINDS of failures, which should predict different Schankian repair primitives. This decomposes scalar surprisal into 7 independent channels, extending Kumar et al.'s event segmentation work from 'where are the boundaries?' to 'what TYPE of boundary is this?' The experiment is straightforward and uses existing infrastructure from both the Darkness Visible and Operator Discovery projects.
